hook.text: A single thunderstorm releases energy equivalent to a 20-kiloton nuclear bomb. Nature's fury in a cloud.

buildup.text: Warm, moist air rises rapidly, cools, and forms towering cumulonimbus clouds up to 60,000 feet tall.

discovery.text: Inside the cloud, updrafts and downdrafts create electrical charge separation—positive on top, negative below.

twist.text: Lightning heats the air to 30,000°F—five times hotter than the sun's surface—in milliseconds.

climax.text: The rapid heating expands air explosively, creating the shockwave we hear as thunder.

punchline.text: Hot air rises, charges separate, 30,000°F strikes. Thunder is the shockwave.

quiz.question: How hot does lightning heat the surrounding air?
